questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a oscilloscope trace to check the key physics idea. A wave has a period of 0.3 s. What is its frequency?
options: 5 Hz
questions.explanation: Frequency is the reciprocal of period.
questions.senNote: Frequency is the reciprocal of period.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe the image formed by a convex lens
questions.question: A learner uses a convex lens ray diagram to check the key physics idea. A convex lens forms an image of a distant object. Which description is correct?
options: The image is always virtual and behind the lens.
The rays never meet after passing through the lens.
The focal point is on the same side as the object.
The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.
questions.correctAnswer: The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.
questions.explanation: A convex lens can form a real image that can be projected onto a screen when the object is beyond the focal length.
questions.senNote: A convex lens can form a real image that can be projected onto a screen when the object is beyond the focal length.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe the image formed by a convex lens
questions.question: A learner uses a camera lens explanation to check the key physics idea. A convex lens forms an image of a distant object. Which description is correct?
options: The image is always virtual and behind the lens.
The rays never meet after passing through the lens.
The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.
A convex lens always spreads parallel rays apart.
questions.correctAnswer: The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.
questions.explanation: A convex lens can form a real image that can be projected onto a screen when the object is beyond the focal length.
questions.senNote: A convex lens can form a real image that can be projected onto a screen when the object is beyond the focal length.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe the image formed by a convex lens
questions.question: A learner uses a distant-object image practical to check the key physics idea. A convex lens forms an image of a distant object. Which description is correct?
options: The image is always virtual and behind the lens.
The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.
The rays never meet after passing through the lens.
The image forms only if the object touches the lens.
questions.correctAnswer: The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.
questions.explanation: A convex lens can form a real image that can be projected onto a screen when the object is beyond the focal length.
questions.senNote: A convex lens can form a real image that can be projected onto a screen when the object is beyond the focal length.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe the image formed by a convex lens
questions.question: A learner uses a focal point demonstration to check the key physics idea. A convex lens forms an image of a distant object. Which description is correct?
options: The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.
The image is always virtual and behind the lens.
The rays never meet after passing through the lens.
The lens changes light into sound.
questions.correctAnswer: The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.
questions.explanation: A convex lens can form a real image that can be projected onto a screen when the object is beyond the focal length.
questions.senNote: A convex lens can form a real image that can be projected onto a screen when the object is beyond the focal length.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe the image formed by a convex lens
questions.question: A learner uses a convex lens ray diagram to prepare for an exam-style question. A convex lens forms an image of a distant object. Which description is correct? Choose the best answer.
options: The image is always virtual and behind the lens.
The rays never meet after passing through the lens.
The image is formed by reflection from the lens surface only.
The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.
questions.correctAnswer: The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.
questions.explanation: A convex lens can form a real image that can be projected onto a screen when the object is beyond the focal length.
questions.senNote: A convex lens can form a real image that can be projected onto a screen when the object is beyond the focal length.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.skill: Describe the image formed by a convex lens
questions.question: A learner uses a camera lens explanation to prepare for an exam-style question. A convex lens forms an image of a distant object. Which description is correct? Choose the best answer.
options: The image is always virtual and behind the lens.
The rays never meet after passing through the lens.
The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.
Parallel rays pass through without changing direction.
questions.correctAnswer: The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.
questions.explanation: A convex lens can form a real image that can be projected onto a screen when the object is beyond the focal length.
questions.senNote: A convex lens can form a real image that can be projected onto a screen when the object is beyond the focal length.

options: The image is always virtual and behind the lens.

questions.subject: Physics

questions.question: A learner uses a focal point demonstration to prepare for an exam-style question. A convex lens forms an image of a distant object. Which description is correct?
options: The image is always virtual and behind the lens.
The rays never meet after passing through the lens.
The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.
The image is always larger than the object.

questions.question: A learner uses a convex lens ray diagram to test a common misconception. A convex lens forms an image of a distant object. Which description is correct?
options: The image is always virtual and behind the lens.
The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.
The rays never meet after passing through the lens.
The rays meet at the centre of the lens material.
questions.correctAnswer: The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.
questions.explanation: A convex lens can form a real image that can be projected onto a screen when the object is beyond the focal length.
questions.senNote: A convex lens can form a real image that can be projected onto a screen when the object is beyond the focal length.

options: The image is real and can be focused onto a screen.

The lens blocks all rays except the central ray.

questions.explanation: A convex lens can form a real image that can be projected onto a screen when the object is beyond the focal length.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a plane mirror ray diagram to check the key physics idea. A ray of light hits a mirror at an angle of incidence of 25°. What is the angle of reflection?
options: The angle of reflection is always zero.
The angle of reflection is twice the angle of incidence.
The angle is measured from the mirror surface only.
questions.explanation: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence.
questions.senNote: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a light ray practical to check the key physics idea. A ray of light hits a mirror at an angle of incidence of 30°. What is the angle of reflection?
options: The angle of reflection is always zero.
The angle of reflection is twice the angle of incidence.
The reflected ray travels back along the normal.
questions.explanation: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence.
questions.senNote: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a reflection law worksheet to check the key physics idea. A ray of light hits a mirror at an angle of incidence of 35°. What is the angle of reflection?
options: The angle of reflection is always zero.
The angle of reflection is twice the angle of incidence.
The angle of incidence changes the mirror into a lens.
questions.explanation: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence.
questions.senNote: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a mirror angle measurement to check the key physics idea. A ray of light hits a mirror at an angle of incidence of 40°. What is the angle of reflection?
options: The angle of reflection is always zero.
The angle of reflection is twice the angle of incidence.
The normal is parallel to the mirror surface.
questions.explanation: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence.
questions.senNote: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a plane mirror ray diagram to prepare for an exam-style question. A ray of light hits a mirror at an angle of incidence of 45°. What is the angle of reflection?
options: The angle of reflection is always zero.
The angle of reflection is twice the angle of incidence.
The angle of reflection is half the incidence angle.
questions.explanation: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence.
questions.senNote: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a light ray practical to prepare for an exam-style question. A ray of light hits a mirror at an angle of incidence of 50°. What is the angle of reflection?
options: The angle of reflection is always zero.
The angle of reflection is twice the angle of incidence.
The ray is absorbed instead of reflected.
questions.explanation: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence.
questions.senNote: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence.

questions.subject: Physics

options: The angle of reflection is always zero.
The angle of reflection is twice the angle of incidence.
The reflected ray must bend towards the normal.
questions.explanation: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence.
questions.senNote: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a mirror angle measurement to prepare for an exam-style question. A ray of light hits a mirror at an angle of incidence of 55°. What is the angle of reflection?
options: The angle of reflection is always zero.
The angle of reflection is twice the angle of incidence.
The angle depends only on the colour of light.
questions.explanation: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence.
questions.senNote: The angle of reflection equals the angle of incidence.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a speaker amplitude demonstration to check the key physics idea. Which change makes a sound louder?
options: Decrease the amplitude.
Decrease the frequency.
Increase the wavelength only.
Increasing the amplitude of the wave
questions.correctAnswer: Increasing the amplitude of the wave
questions.explanation: Loudness depends on amplitude. A larger amplitude means more energy is transferred.
questions.senNote: Loudness depends on amplitude. A larger amplitude means more energy is transferred.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a sound sensor trace to check the key physics idea. Which change makes a sound louder?
options: Decrease the amplitude.
Decrease the frequency.
Increasing the amplitude of the wave
Change the wave from longitudinal to transverse.
questions.correctAnswer: Increasing the amplitude of the wave
questions.explanation: Loudness depends on amplitude. A larger amplitude means more energy is transferred.
questions.senNote: Loudness depends on amplitude. A larger amplitude means more energy is transferred.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a volume comparison example to check the key physics idea. Which change makes a sound louder?
options: Decrease the amplitude.
Increasing the amplitude of the wave
Decrease the frequency.
Remove the particles from the medium.
questions.correctAnswer: Increasing the amplitude of the wave
questions.explanation: Loudness depends on amplitude. A larger amplitude means more energy is transferred.
questions.senNote: Loudness depends on amplitude. A larger amplitude means more energy is transferred.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a oscilloscope sound display to check the key physics idea. Which change makes a sound louder?
options: Increasing the amplitude of the wave
Decrease the amplitude.
Decrease the frequency.
Lower the energy carried by the wave.
questions.correctAnswer: Increasing the amplitude of the wave
questions.explanation: Loudness depends on amplitude. A larger amplitude means more energy is transferred.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: A learner uses a slinky wave demonstration to check the key physics idea. Which statement about transverse and longitudinal waves is correct?
options: All waves are transverse in air.
Sound waves are transverse and light waves are longitudinal.
Light waves need particles to carry them.
Light waves are transverse, but sound waves in air are longitudinal.
questions.correctAnswer: Light waves are transverse, but sound waves in air are longitudinal.
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave. Sound in air is longitudinal.
questions.senNote: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave. Sound in air is longitudinal.

options: All waves are transverse in air.

questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave. Sound in air is longitudinal.
questions.senNote: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave. Sound in air is longitudinal.

options: All waves are transverse in air.

Longitudinal waves have vibrations at right angles to travel.
questions.correctAnswer: Light waves are transverse, but sound waves in air are longitudinal.
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave. Sound in air is longitudinal.
questions.senNote: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave. Sound in air is longitudinal.

questions.correctAnswer: Light waves are transverse, but sound waves in air are longitudinal.
